sections.title: Know Your Risk
sections.body: Understand whether your home or workplace is in a flood-prone area. Check FEMA flood maps and your local emergency management website for zone designations.
tips: Look up your address on the FEMA Flood Map Service Center (msc.fema.gov).
Sign up for local emergency alerts (Wireless Emergency Alerts, CodeRED, or your county system).
Know the elevation of your property relative to nearby water bodies.
Identify the nearest high ground and multiple evacuation routes.

sections.title: Prepare an Emergency Kit
sections.body: Assemble a go-bag that can sustain your household for at least 72 hours without outside support.
tips: Water: 1 gallon per person per day for at least 3 days.
Non-perishable food for 3 days, plus a manual can opener.
First-aid kit, prescription medications, and copies of medical records.
Flashlights, extra batteries, and a battery-powered or hand-crank radio.
Copies of important documents in a waterproof container (IDs, insurance, deeds).
Cash in small bills — ATMs may be offline.
Phone charger and portable power bank.

sections.title: Protect Your Property
sections.body: Take steps before flood season to reduce damage to your home and belongings.
tips: Install check valves in plumbing to prevent floodwater backflow.
Elevate electrical panels, appliances, and valuables above the expected flood level.
Keep gutters and drains clear of debris.
Consider flood insurance — standard homeowner policies do NOT cover flood damage.
Photograph or video your possessions for insurance purposes and store the file off-site.

sections.title: If a Flood Watch or Warning Is Issued
sections.body: A Watch means flooding is possible; a Warning means it is happening or imminent. Act immediately on a Warning.
tips: Move immediately to higher ground — do not wait for official orders if you feel threatened.
Disconnect electrical appliances if safe to do so, but do not touch them while standing in water.
Avoid walking in moving water — just 6 inches can knock an adult down.
Never drive through flooded roads. 'Turn Around, Don't Drown.' 2 feet of water can carry away most vehicles.
If trapped in a building, go to the highest floor. Signal for help from a window, not the roof.

sections.title: Evacuating Safely
sections.body: If authorities order or recommend evacuation, leave as early as possible.
tips: Follow designated evacuation routes — shortcuts may be flooded.
Take your go-bag and important documents.
Let a family member or friend outside the area know your destination.
Bring pets — many emergency shelters have separate pet areas.
Lock your home and turn off utilities if instructed.

sections.title: Returning Home Safely
sections.body: Re-enter only when authorities declare it safe. Floodwaters and damaged structures pose serious hazards.
tips: Do not enter if structural damage is visible or water is still present inside.
Use a flashlight — do not use open flames; gas leaks are common after floods.
Have a licensed electrician inspect your home before restoring power.
Wear rubber boots and waterproof gloves — floodwater is often contaminated with sewage and chemicals.
Document all damage with photos and video before cleaning up, for insurance claims.

sections.title: Cleaning Up and Health
sections.body: Floodwater carries bacteria, mold spores, and hazardous materials. Protect your health during cleanup.
tips: Discard any food that may have come into contact with floodwater.
Boil water for drinking and cooking until authorities lift the boil-water advisory.
Remove wet materials within 24–48 hours to prevent mold growth.
Use N95 masks and eye protection when removing debris or mold.
Seek mental health support — flooding is traumatic; community resources are available.

sections.title: Filing for Assistance
sections.body: Financial and logistical help is available from multiple sources after a declared disaster.
tips: Register with FEMA at DisasterAssistance.gov or call 1-800-621-3362.
Contact your insurance agent immediately to begin the claims process.
Keep all receipts for emergency expenses — they may be reimbursable.
Check with local nonprofits (Red Cross, Salvation Army) for immediate needs.
Apply for Small Business Administration (SBA) low-interest disaster loans if eligible.
